perturbation.original_text: Joint Venture Company shall fulfill all sales of Products under all Transferred Contracts, shall invoice Authorized Customers for all sales of Products under all Transferred Contracts, and shall use commercially reasonable efforts to collect the amounts due under such invoices.
perturbation.changed_text: Joint Venture Company will endeavor to fulfill sales of Products under all Transferred Contracts, may invoice Authorized Customers for sales of Products under all Transferred Contracts, and shall attempt to collect the amounts due under such invoices.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'shall fulfill' with 'will endeavor to fulfill,' 'shall invoice' with 'may invoice,' and 'shall use commercially reasonable efforts' with 'shall attempt to collect.' This weakens the obligation to fulfill sales, invoice customers, and collect payments, making these actions discretionary rather than mandatory.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of contract - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By weakening the language around fulfilling sales, invoicing, and collecting payments, the modified text introduces ambiguity that could be interpreted as a failure to act in good faith, especially if Joint Venture Company consistently fails to fulfill these obligations, invoice correctly, or collect payments diligently. Delaware courts interpret the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing as requiring a party to refrain from arbitrary or unreasonable conduct that prevents the other party from receiving the fruits of the contract.

General Provisions Part 3 Territorial Applicability and General Rules Â§ 1-301. Territorial applicability; partiesâ power to choose applicable law. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this State and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this State or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106; (3) Section 4-102; (4) Section 4A-507; (5) Section 5-116; (6) Section 8-110; (7) Sections 9-301 through 9-307; (8) Section 12-107. 5A Del. The parties, by agreement, may determine the standards by which the performance of those obliga
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by changing 'shall' to 'will endeavor to' and 'may,' and 'shall use commercially reasonable efforts' to 'shall attempt to collect.' This weakens the mandatory nature of fulfilling sales, invoicing customers, and collecting payments. The Law Explanation argues that this could be interpreted as a failure to act in good faith under Delaware law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Â§ 1-302 (b), states that 'The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement.' While the changed text doesn't explicitly disclaim these obligations, it weakens them to the point where they are arguably not being met, creating a contradiction because the original text was a stronger guarantee of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: Loop shall have the sole and exclusive right to enter into agreements for the sale of Loop-branded Products or any Products produced using Licensed Subject Matter, and the exclusive right to set the price and quantity to be supplied under each Loop Sales Contract and other terms and conditions as determined by Loop in its commercially reasonable discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: Loop may enter into agreements for the sale of Loop-branded Products or any Products produced using Licensed Subject Matter, and has the right to generally suggest the price and quantity to be supplied under each Loop Sales Contract and other terms and conditions as casually considered by Loop.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'shall have the sole and exclusive right' with 'may enter into agreements,' and 'exclusive right to set the price and quantity' with 'right to generally suggest the price and quantity,' removing exclusivity and introducing vagueness. The change of 'commercially reasonable discretion' to 'casually considered by Loop' makes the standards by which Loop sets terms and conditions much less strict.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Duty of Care
perturbation.law_explanation: Although Delaware does not explicitly recognize a general duty of care in contract law as in tort law, the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to act with a certain level of care and reasonableness in performing their contractual obligations. By changing the language to 'casually considered by Loop,' it implies a lack of due diligence and care in setting prices and conditions, potentially allowing Loop to act arbitrarily, which could be seen as a breach of the implied covenant of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 2 Authenticated PDF Sales Part 1. Short Title, General Construction and Subject Matter Part 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract Part 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract Part 4. Title, Creditors and Good Faith Purchases Part 5. Performance Part 6. Breach, Repudiation and Excuse Part 7. Remedies
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities by replacing 'shall have the sole and exclusive right' with 'may enter into agreements' and 'exclusive right to set the price and quantity' with 'right to generally suggest the price and quantity,' thereby weakening the enforceability of the original obligations. Furthermore, the change from 'commercially reasonable discretion' to 'casually considered by Loop' significantly lowers the standard of care expected. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Good Faith' but is not explicit on the standard of care required, the 'Law Explanation' suggests this falls short of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law, because Delaware requires parties to act with a certain level of care and reasonableness in performing their contractual obligations. The modified text creates an Ambiguous Legal Obligation, satisfying the condition for contradiction under the 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall implement reasonable procedures to prohibit the unauthorized disclo-sure or misuse of the other Party's Confidential Information and shall not intentionally disclose such Confidential Information to any third party except as may be necessary or useful in connection with the rights and obligations of such Party under this Agreement, and subject to confidentiality obligations similar to those set forth in this Article 4. Each of the Parties shall use at least the same procedures and degree of care that it uses to prevent the disclosure of its own confidential information of like importance to prevent the disclosure of Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other Party under this Agreement, but in no event less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will try to implement procedures to discourage the unauthorized disclosure of the other Party's Confidential Information and will avoid intentionally disclosing such Confidential Information to any third party to the best of its understanding of what is necessary in connection with the rights and obligations of such Party under this Agreement. Each of the Parties will aim to use similar procedures and care that it uses for its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification replaces 'shall implement reasonable procedures' with 'will try to implement procedures to discourage', 'shall not intentionally disclose' with 'will avoid intentionally disclosing to the best of its understanding', and 'shall use at least the same procedures and degree of care' with 'will aim to use similar procedures and care'. This weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, introducing significant ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The federal Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) provides a cause of action for the misappropriation of trade secrets. The original text creates a clear obligation to protect confidential information. By weakening the language, the modified text creates ambiguity about the level of protection required. If, as a result of the ambiguous language, a party fails to adequately protect confidential information, leading to its misappropriation, this could potentially conflict with the DTSA, as the DTSA imposes liability for misappropriation, which includes acquiring a trade secret by improper means or disclosing it without consent when there is a duty to maintain its secrecy.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The original contract language uses 'shall implement', 'shall not', and 'shall use', creating clear obligations. The modified language weakens these obligations by using 'will try to implement', 'will avoid...to the best of its understanding', and 'will aim to use', introducing ambiguity and discretion. The Law Explanation states that this weakened language could lead to inadequate protection of confidential information and potential conflict with the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings for misappropriation of trade secrets, suggesting that failure to adequately protect such secrets can lead to legal action. By making the obligation to protect confidential information ambiguous, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation and potential legal conflict under 18 USC 1836.
